---
title: "Open Research Agenda and Bibliography Plan"
chapter_id: "open-research-agenda-and-bibliography-plan"
part_id: "evidence-implementation-living-book"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-27"
primary_sources:
  - "verification_bandwidth"
  - "benchmaxxing"
  - "alignment_field"
  - "scf"
  - "planforge"
  - "vcm_public"
  - "spinoza"
  - "talos"
  - "rmi"
  - "cgs"
  - "genesiscode"
  - "simulation_scaling"
  - "moecot"
  - "coilmoecot"
  - "road_to_agi"
  - "vcm_editable"
  - "field_of_god_ai_constitution"
  - "project_theseus_whitepaper"
  - "circle_calculus_core"
  - "circle_ai_contract_suite"
  - "theseus_circle_transfer"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Appendix C now records passage-reviewed mappings for all twenty-one assigned sources, including source-note-only connector/recovery sources and pinned local Project Theseus/Circle/constitution references; support remains argument until external literature normalization, direct citation checks, new-paper triage rehearsals, artifact reproduction, or accepted evidence transitions justify promotion."
  - "The research_backlog_record and new_paper_triage_scenario fixtures and AsiStackProofs.BibliographyPlan finite-record predicates validate backlog-record shape, triage state, source storage policy, public-safety state, chapter action policy, chapter-decision refs, deduplication state, merge/split policy, boundary-decision rationale, required pre-drafting work, evidence-transition preconditions, source refs, promotion blockers, support-state effect, non-claims, source-note gates, chapter-assignment gates, and synthetic update/propose/defer/reject decisions only; they do not prove citation accuracy, external-literature completeness, claim-to-source mapping quality, public-release permission, or real new-paper triage quality."
  - "Connector/recovery and variant sources remain bounded: MoECOT, CoilMoECOT, Road To AGI, and VCM Editable do not promote runtime, benchmark, model-facing, or citation-normalized third-party claims."
  - "No support-state promotion, external-literature normalization result, direct citation check, benchmark reproduction, runtime artifact, model-quality result, Circle command, external Circle build, current Theseus report verification, policy-engine result, or raw source publication is implied by this drafting pass."
---

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `open-research-agenda-and-bibliography-plan` |
| Part | Part IV - Evidence, Implementation, and the Living Book |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-27 |
| Primary source records | `verification_bandwidth`, `benchmaxxing`, `alignment_field`, `scf`, `planforge`, `vcm_public`, `spinoza`, `talos`, `rmi`, `cgs`, `genesiscode`, `simulation_scaling`, `moecot`, `coilmoecot`, `road_to_agi`, `vcm_editable`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution`, `project_theseus_whitepaper`, `circle_calculus_core`, `circle_ai_contract_suite`, `theseus_circle_transfer` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `verification_bandwidth`, `benchmaxxing`; supporting: `alignment_field`, `scf`, `planforge`, `vcm_public`, `spinoza`, `talos`, `rmi`, `cgs`, `genesiscode`, `simulation_scaling`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution`, `project_theseus_whitepaper`, `circle_calculus_core`, `circle_ai_contract_suite`, `theseus_circle_transfer`; connector/recovery: `moecot`, `coilmoecot`, `road_to_agi`, `vcm_editable` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `verification_bandwidth`, `benchmaxxing`, `alignment_field`, `scf`, `planforge`, `vcm_public`, `spinoza`, `talos`, `rmi`, `cgs`, `genesiscode`, `simulation_scaling`, `moecot`, `coilmoecot`, `road_to_agi`, `vcm_editable`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution`, `project_theseus_whitepaper`, `circle_calculus_core`, `circle_ai_contract_suite`, `theseus_circle_transfer`; raw cache: `verification_bandwidth`, `benchmaxxing`, `alignment_field`, `scf`, `planforge`, `vcm_public`, `spinoza`, `talos`, `rmi`, `cgs`, `genesiscode`, `simulation_scaling`; source-note-only or connector/recovery: `moecot`, `coilmoecot`, `road_to_agi`, `vcm_editable`; pinned local references: Project Theseus, Circle Math, and Field of God AI Constitution |
| Test state | Source inventory validation, source-note validation, `research_backlog_record.valid.json`, and `new_paper_triage_scenario.valid.json` protocol fixture validation pass; the backlog fixture carries triage state, source storage policy, public-safety state, chapter action policy, chapter-decision refs, deduplication state, merge/split policy, boundary rationale, required pre-drafting work, evidence-transition preconditions, source refs, promotion blockers, support-state effect, and non-claims; the triage scenario fixture covers synthetic update-existing, propose-new-chapter, defer-external-literature, and reject-duplicate cases only; `AsiStackProofs.BibliographyPlan` implements finite source-derived-claim ingestion and source-assignment gates. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter manages future work. It does not cite unread sources, infer bibliography metadata, or promote missing/private artifacts as evidence.

The previous chapter defines how the living book changes without losing evidence control. This final chapter keeps that change honest after publication: every missing source, external-literature queue, proof target, experiment idea, artifact reproduction task, and possible chapter insertion becomes a backlog item with an owner boundary instead of a vague promise that future work will fill the gap.

The agenda is also admission control for future enthusiasm. A new paper, benchmark result, tool, proof idea, or conversation-mined insight can improve the book, but it should not enter as undifferentiated confidence. It enters as a source record, source note, chapter assignment, claim mapping, proof or test route, and residual.

::: {.asi-human-only}
## Human Reading Path

The final chapter keeps the book honest after the reader reaches the end. It names what remains missing: sources to load, literature to normalize, experiments to run, proofs to refine, schemas to harden, and claims that must not rise above their current support state.

For a human reader, this is not an apology for incompleteness. It is the living-book contract in its most explicit form. The architecture can keep improving only if open work stays visible instead of being smoothed away by confident prose.
:::

## Problem

A living book can decay into a pile of sources unless it has a research backlog. Future writing agents need to know which source to load, which chapter owns the boundary, which evidence is missing, which proof target is operational, and when a new paper should update an existing chapter instead of creating a duplicate one.

They also need to know what not to do. Do not cite a title because it sounds relevant. Do not add a chapter because a source uses new language for an old boundary. Do not promote a claim because a private artifact exists somewhere else. Do not turn an external reference named inside a draft into a citation until the reference itself has been checked.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

A pile of sources or ad hoc citations does not tell future writing agents what to load, compare, prove, test, or defer. It also creates citation drift: a chapter may cite a source it has not actually mined, or a new source may be treated as evidence before it has an inventory record and source note.

The bibliography for this book is not just a list of references at the end. It is an operating surface for a living research program. Each source has to answer practical questions: which chapter owns it, which claim can it inform, what text has actually been read, what remains private or connector-only, what external literature needs direct verification, and what would have to happen before the source could raise support above `argument`. A static bibliography cannot carry that burden by itself.

The research agenda is the matching map of ignorance. It names the work the book is not yet allowed to pretend is done: citation normalization, benchmark reproduction, semantic proof adequacy, chapter-level tests, external literature review, artifact import, reader-edition review, and new-paper triage. Keeping that ignorance explicit is what lets the book keep growing without becoming more confident merely because it became longer.

The book therefore needs more than bibliography management. It needs state management for knowledge work: unread, inventoried, cached, source-noted, mapped to claims, passage-reviewed, integrated into chapter prose, tested, mechanized, reproduced, deprecated, or refuted. Those states are not decoration. They are the difference between a living book and a confident scrapbook.

## Core Claim

[open-research-agenda-and-bibliography-plan.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] The bibliography and research agenda should map source families, external literature queues, missing papers, proof targets, experiment backlogs, and chapter insertion rules.

The claim remains at `argument` support. The repository now has source inventory, source notes, chapter listings, exact core-claim source-note mappings, and passage-reviewed mapping refs for the assigned sources, but external literature normalization, direct citation checks, artifact reproduction, and new-paper triage rehearsals remain incomplete.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C now records twenty-one passage-reviewed mappings for this claim. The review turns the agenda into a context-loading map for future writing, proof, and experiment work; it does not turn any backlog item into completed evidence.

| Source group | Reviewed support | Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| Verification, evidence, and benchmark backlog: `verification_bandwidth`, `benchmaxxing`, `spinoza` | Context-adequacy tests, semantic-unit verification, contradiction-rate proposals, benchmark lifecycle, residuals, ledgers, anti-Goodhart safeguards, proof-carrying claims, belief revision, downgrade paths, and protected axioms. | No contradiction-rate test, theorem result, benchmark run, mutation/holdout run, contamination audit, verifier-quality result, autoformalization result, or citation-normalized literature claim. |
| Governance, replacement, and safety backlog: `alignment_field`, `scf`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution` | Normative assumptions, value conflict, dignity, corrigibility, stable capability identity, evaluator boundaries, authority ceilings, lifecycle ordering, rollback, recovery, constitutional tool/memory/self-improvement constraints, and least sufficient power. | No empirical consciousness, moral-status, alignment, rights-preservation, route/evaluator correctness, replacement safety, runtime policy-engine, red-team, system-prompt, or moral-correctness result. |
| Planning, context, execution, and improvement backlog: `planforge`, `vcm_public`, `talos`, `rmi`, `genesiscode` | Planning experiments, primitive schemas, VCM packet schemas, source binding, typed job lifecycles, audit/replay, residual escrow, specialist lifecycle, capability policies, effect logs, replay, and translation-validation topics. | No planner implementation, VCM-Bench, retrieval-quality result, Talos runtime/security run, RMI prototype, GenesisCode implementation, replay checker, benchmark, or security audit. |
| Compression, simulation, and optional substrate backlog: `cgs`, `simulation_scaling`, `coilmoecot`, `vcm_editable` | Compact-seed and residual-honesty research items, simulation contracts and resource limits, optional routed coil/specialist lanes, richer VCM terminology, planner-guided paging, and third-party citation candidates. | No CGS benchmark, simulation benchmark, feasibility audit, CoilMoECOT baseline, route run, VCM model-facing result, or normalized third-party citation. |
| Implementation and proof-contract backlog: `moecot`, `road_to_agi`, `project_theseus_whitepaper`, `circle_calculus_core`, `circle_ai_contract_suite`, `theseus_circle_transfer` | Runtime artifact tracking, readiness gates, replay, promotion blockers, remaining-work status, report-first Theseus artifacts, theorem ids, proof-boundary discipline, AI contract fields, receipts, consumer gates, and transfer-lane insertion rules. | No MoECOT runtime artifact, reproduced command, current Theseus report verification, external Circle build, Circle command, generated contract pack, receipt replay, transfer consumer, proxy benchmark, private workload report, or model-improvement result. |

## Mechanism

The research agenda is a queue with admission rules. Verification Bandwidth supplies context-adequacy and contradiction-rate test ideas. Benchmaxxing supplies benchmark lifecycle and anti-Goodhart backlog structure. SCF, VCM, PlanForge, Talos, Spinoza, RMI, CGS, GenesisCode, Simulation Scaling, Circle, Theseus, MoECOT, and the constitutional sources each name future artifacts that could strengthen the book only after source notes, direct citation checks, proof/test routes, and non-claim boundaries are recorded.

The Research Backlog Record keeps those future moves from becoming loose intentions. It should say whether the item is discovered, inventoried, source-noted, mapped, integrated, deferred, blocked, rejected, or retired; where the source text is allowed to live; whether the public surface is safe, redacted, permission-gated, private, or blocked; what chapter action policy applies; which chapter-decision refs justify that policy; whether the item is unique, overlapping, duplicate, superseded, or blocked; why the boundary decision was made; what must happen before drafting; what evidence-transition preconditions would have to be met; which source refs were inspected; what still blocks promotion; and what support-state effect is allowed.

A good backlog item is more specific than "research this." It names the source or gap, the affected chapter boundary, the evidence state it could change, the proof or test route that would make the change meaningful, and the non-claims that remain even after the work is done. That structure prevents two common mistakes. The first is duplicate expansion, where every new paper becomes a new chapter even though it belongs inside an existing boundary. The second is evidence smearing, where a useful source influences prose in five places but no one can say which claim it actually supports.

The useful lifecycle is conservative: discover, inventory, access, note, assign, map, draft, verify, integrate, release. A source can stop at any stage. A missing PDF can remain a backlog item. A useful but unread reference can remain external literature. A source-note-only connector item can inform planning while remaining outside public evidence. A test idea can wait until a fixture or harness exists. The system is allowed to remember unfinished work without pretending it is done.

New AI papers should enter through this same gate. If a paper gives better vocabulary for an existing layer, it should update that layer's source queue and source note. If it introduces a genuinely new interface, invariant, artifact type, or failure mode that no chapter owns, it can justify a new chapter. If it is interesting but not yet read, it belongs in the external literature queue, not in the claim/evidence matrix. The rule keeps the book dynamic without making it shapeless.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["New source or gap"] --> B["Inventory record"]
  B --> C["Source note / access status"]
  C --> D{"Existing boundary?"}
  D -- "yes" --> E["Update chapter source queue"]
  D -- "no" --> F["Propose precise chapter"]
  E --> G["Claim/evidence mapping"]
  F --> G
  G --> H["Proof/test/backlog item"]
```

The record should track source id, access state, source storage policy, public-safety state, assigned chapters, chapter-decision refs, deduplication state, merge/split policy, source-note state, claim-mapping state, passage-review state, external-literature need, proof/test backlog, pre-drafting requirements, insertion decision, promotion blockers, and residuals.

## Interfaces

The interface is the Research Backlog Record.

Minimum fields:

- `backlog_id`
- `triage_state`
- `source_or_gap`
- `access_state`
- `source_storage_policy`
- `public_safety_state`
- `assigned_chapters`
- `chapter_action_policy`
- `chapter_decision_refs`
- `deduplication_state`
- `merge_or_split_policy`
- `boundary_decision_rationale`
- `source_note_state`
- `claim_mapping_state`
- `passage_review_state`
- `external_literature_need`
- `proof_or_test_backlog`
- `evidence_transition_precondition`
- `required_before_drafting`
- `insertion_decision`
- `source_refs`
- `support_state_effect`
- `promotion_blockers`
- `residuals`
- `next_action`
- `non_claims`

Appendix G lists the corpus and external literature queue. Source notes support drafting. `docs/book_outline.md` tells future agents what to mine. `book_structure.json` prevents chapter-order drift. Appendix C records support-state boundaries.

Those interfaces are deliberately redundant. The outline tells a writing agent what context to load. The manifest tells Quarto and validation scripts where the chapter belongs. Source notes tell a reviewer what was actually mined. Appendix C tells readers which claim-state boundary is currently justified. Appendix G keeps the broader corpus visible. A living book needs this redundancy because any one surface can become stale; the validation loop and changelog make drift visible before it becomes authority.

When a future agent receives a new paper, the interface should produce a triage decision before prose changes: update existing chapter, propose new chapter, route to appendix, keep in backlog, or reject as out of scope. The decision should name the boundary it affects and the evidence it could change. That makes dynamic chapters possible without forcing manual renumbering or turning the outline into a catchall.

## Invariants

- Do not cite unread sources as support.
- Do not infer bibliographic metadata.
- New papers update inventory before chapter claims.
- Missing sources are backlog items, not evidence.
- New chapters are added only when they own a distinct boundary.

A source may inspire design language before it supports a claim only if the text says so and the evidence state remains unchanged. Inspiration, author intent, source-derived support, implementation evidence, and reproduced results are different states. The agenda exists to keep them from merging.

## Failure modes

- Citation drift.
- Duplicate chapter creation for overlapping ideas.
- Missing sources treated as evidence.
- Private empirical results leaking into public claims.
- External references named by source documents becoming citations before direct verification.

Another failure is backlog rot. If every uncertain item becomes permanent "future work," the research agenda stops guiding action. Backlog records should keep next actions small enough to execute: fetch source, write note, verify citation, add fixture, run benchmark, formalize finite predicate, update chapter, or close as out of scope.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The research agenda starts as a research backlog record. The repository fixture makes future source ingestion and chapter insertion reviewable, including triage state, source storage policy, public-safety state, chapter action policy, chapter-decision refs, deduplication state, merge/split policy, boundary rationale, required pre-drafting work, evidence-transition preconditions, source refs, promotion blockers, support-state effect, and non-claims, but it does not normalize external literature, perform direct citation checks, run a new-paper scenario, or promote mapped claims to stronger support states.

The next useful implementation step is a live rehearsal against a user-provided AI paper or artifact. The synthetic fixture set now covers update-existing, propose-new-chapter, defer-external-literature, and reject-duplicate decisions, but it deliberately avoids claiming that any particular future source has been evaluated.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The logical end state is a source and research graph for the entire ASI Stack. The bibliography would not be an afterthought at the end of the book. It would be the routing table that tells future agents which ideas have sources, which sources have been read, which chapters they affect, which proof or experiment backlogs they create, and which claims still cannot move.

At that mature boundary, every paper, project, local note, external reference, conversation-mined packet, recovery item, and missing source would have a public-safety policy, ingestion state, source note, chapter assignments, deduplication status, evidence-transition preconditions, proof/test backlog, citation status, and non-claims. New material would update the research graph before it changes manuscript prose.

The mature product surface would include:

- A normalized source inventory tied to source notes, Appendix G, chapter source queues, claim/evidence mappings, proof targets, experiment backlog, and release records.
- Intake scenarios for update-existing, propose-new-chapter, defer-external-literature, merge/split, reject-duplicate, supersede-variant, recover-missing-source, and block-private-source cases.
- Citation verification that distinguishes direct reading, source-note review, metadata-only external context, private/local context, connector-only access, and unread references.
- Research backlog records with next actions small enough to execute: fetch, cache, read, write note, verify citation, add fixture, formalize predicate, run benchmark, update chapter, or close.
- Chapter-boundary governance so papers are mined for architecture interfaces and failure modes rather than converted one-to-one into chapters.
- Failure closure for citation drift, duplicate chapter creation, bibliographic hallucination, private result leakage, external-reference laundering, and backlog rot.

In that final product, the bibliography would be active infrastructure: it would tell the book what it is allowed to know and what work must happen next. The source graph remains a target architecture until source-intake runs, citation checks, evidence-transition reviews, and backlog closures show that the research plan can update the book without turning unread material into support.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Research backlog record fixture validation | Validate that a backlog record names source/gap, triage state, access state, source storage policy, public-safety state, assigned chapters, chapter action policy, chapter-decision refs, deduplication state, merge/split policy, boundary rationale, source-note state, claim mapping state, external-literature need, proof/test backlog, required pre-drafting work, evidence-transition preconditions, insertion decision, source refs, promotion blockers, support-state effect, residuals, next action, and non-claims. | implemented; passing via `python3 scripts/validate_protocol_examples.py` |
| Source inventory validation test | Check that assigned source IDs exist before chapter claims use them. | implemented by validation; validated locally |
| Source-note backlog audit | Check which assigned sources still lack usable source notes. | source notes, exact core-claim mappings, and passage-reviewed mapping refs currently exist for assigned sources; external-literature normalization and evidence transitions remain incomplete |
| New-paper triage scenario | Check whether a synthetic intake case updates an existing chapter, proposes a new boundary, defers unread external literature, or rejects a duplicate variant without changing support state. | implemented by protocol validation; validated locally |

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:bibliography.plan.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.BibliographyPlan` | A source-derived claim requires a source note or equivalent ingested-source artifact. | implemented |
| `lean:bibliography.plan.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.BibliographyPlan` | A new source cannot be assigned to a non-existent chapter id. | implemented |

These Lean hooks are implemented as finite-record predicates over source-derived claim and new-source assignment reviews. They prove only the record-level gates: a source-derived claim requires a source note or equivalent ingested-source artifact, and an accepted new-source assignment requires an existing chapter id. They do not prove citation accuracy, external-literature completeness, claim-to-source mapping quality, or new-paper triage quality.

## Source crosswalk

This chapter intentionally keeps a broad source set because it governs the backlog for the whole book. Appendix G remains the generated corpus map. Appendix H remains author-intent and lineage context, not evidence.

Priority backlog:

- Claim-to-mechanism reconciliation beyond this core-claim mapping pass, especially where future chapter drafting depends on narrower mechanisms rather than broad source-family assignment.
- Live new-paper intake rehearsal against a user-provided source after source access, public-safety policy, and source-note permissions are clear.
- External literature normalization and direct citation checks.
- Reproduction or import of any benchmark artifacts before support promotion.
- Lean triage for proof targets that are currently schema or process contracts.
- New-paper triage rules for dynamic chapter insertion.

Recovery boundaries:

- MoECOT, Road To AGI, VCM editable, and CoilMoECOT are readable through connector/source notes, but reported benchmark or runtime claims remain unpromoted.
- Private empirical results must stay out of public claims until artifact, command, environment, and publication permission are recorded.
- External references named inside source documents must be verified directly before becoming bibliography citations.

## Summary

The research agenda is the book's backlog discipline. It keeps source ingestion, bibliography, proof targets, experiments, missing evidence, and chapter insertion rules visible so the living book can grow without losing evidence control.

After the exact core-claim mappings, the next bottleneck is no longer knowing which sources belong to which claims. It is doing the slower work: passage review, external citation normalization, artifact import or reproduction, and source-specific chapter revision without collapsing design rationale into unsupported evidence.

This final chapter is the handoff back to future work. The book can keep changing, but every change should leave a trace: what source moved, what chapter boundary changed, what claim state stayed the same or moved, what test or proof was affected, what edition was regenerated, and what remains blocked.
